title: The Power of Habit: Why We Do What We Do in Life and Business
author: Charles Duhigg
themes: The Golden Rule of Habit Change
excerpt: Charles Duhigg's exploration of the science of habit formation reveals the three-step loop—cue, routine, reward—that governs all habits, and shows how understanding this loop is the key to changing them.
keyIdeas: The habit loop: cue, routine, reward — the neurological structure driving all habits
Craving is the engine of habit formation; habits are built when the brain learns to anticipate rewards
Keystone habits trigger widespread change by starting a chain reaction in other behaviors
The golden rule of habit change: keep the cue and reward, change the routine
keyTakeaways: Habits can be changed if you understand their underlying structure and modify the routine while preserving the cue and reward
Small wins from keystone habits create momentum that transforms entire organizations and lives
Willpower is the single most important keystone habit; strengthening it improves nearly every area of life
Organizational habits shape culture — changing them requires concentrating on pivotal routines
whoShouldRead: Anyone trying to build or break a habit
Managers and leaders seeking organizational transformation
Students of psychology and behavioral science
People curious about why they do what they do
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking a quick 30-day fix without understanding the science
Those who prefer purely academic writing over journalistic storytelling
